schools.summary: Gregory and Augustine allegorise every detail: marriage = the incarnation joining the Son to human nature; oxen/fatlings = OT and NT fathers; the wedding garment = charity.
Chrysostom and Pseudo-Chrysostom read the plain message: the casting out of the Jews, the calling of the Gentiles, and the strictness of life required; 'all things ready' = the table of Scripture.
Origen multiplies senses — king, king's son, oxen as the mightier oracles, fatlings as the sweet — reading the feast as the divine word.
Henry: the gospel feast, a heaven on earth now (Isaiah 25:6), consummated at the marriage of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7).
Robertson and Ellicott: Matthew alone gives this parable, distinct from Luke 14; the plural gamous = the wedding festivities.

debates.question: Is this the same parable as the Great Supper of Luke 14?

sides.position: A distinct parable, as the order and details show
Possibly Matthew's version of Luke's parable relocated
holders: some scholars (noted, not endorsed, by Robertson)

outliers.note: Reads the king, the son, the servants, oxen and fatlings as graded senses of the divine oracles.

schools.summary: King = God, son = Christ, marriage = His union with the Church; the bridal image drawn from Isaiah 62:5 and Jeremiah 3; gamous (plural) = the wedding festivities.
Calvin: the Jews' double favour — election above the nations and the prophets' announcement of their adoption; the guest-list summoned by servants.
Clarke maps the whole allegory (king/son/marriage/feast); Wesley: God made the feast when He brought His first-begotten into the world.
The servants = the prophets up to the Baptist, now the first gospel preachers; Robertson on 'calling the called' and the Jewish custom of a second invitation (Esther 5:8).
Gill: the servants are John the Baptist and the apostles, God's chosen and sent ministers who serve cheerfully.

outliers.note: Calls the leaders' stubborn refusal 'The Hebrew Tragedy,' linking it to John 1:11.

schools.summary: The second servants = the apostles after the resurrection; ariston is properly the morning meal (Robertson); the oxen and fatlings = spiritual blessings under Isaiah's imagery (Isaiah 25:6).
Calvin: the first invitation spans all the prophecies, the second the urgent gospel summons; the people's cruelty grew until it fell on Christ and the apostles.
Henry: 'all things ready' = the Father to accept, the Son to intercede, the Spirit to sanctify, pardon, peace, and comfort all prepared.
Wesley glosses the fatlings as fatted beasts and fowls.

outliers.note: Comments here on the parallel parable of the merciful king and the debtor, off the wedding-feast thread.

schools.summary: On amelēsantes ('neglecting') and the two pursuits, farm and merchandise; a portrait of sinners who value the world above God.
Henry: making light of Christ is the world's damning sin; multitudes perish by mere carelessness; lawful things undo us when unlawfully managed.
Calvin: the Jews wholly devoted to earthly things; lawful business pleaded as an excuse, yet fields and riches are snares binding us to earth.
Wesley: how many perish by misusing lawful things.
Barnes distinguishes silent neglect from positive malice; Ellicott: the word implies wanton outrage, the parable embracing the sufferings of prophets and Christian martyrs alike.
Gill sees the seized servants as the apostles imprisoned after the ascension (Peter and John), distinguishing the careless from the spiteful.
Jerome: the armies are the Romans under Vespasian and Titus who burned Jerusalem; 'the man is dropped and He is called only a King' when He comes to vengeance.
Ellicott and Robertson: 'troops' (strateumata = bands, not grand armies), fulfilled first in Jerusalem's fall, then in all judgments on unbelief; Clarke notes the 'this very king' reading.
Henry: the Roman armies are God's, raised and sent (Isaiah 10:6-7); the crying sin was murdering His servants.
Calvin: the same destruction awaits all who violently oppose the gospel's ministers; Wesley/Gill: the Romans employed of God against the Jews.
Barnes: 'not worthy' = a privilege despised, not a merit lacked (Acts 13:46; Revelation 3:4); the wedding stands ready while they forfeit it.
Clarke: unworthy because they made light of it; an Eastern custom made refusing a feast a breach of God's law; worthiness = coming through the blood of Jesus.
Henry: it is not owing to God that sinners perish but to themselves, like Israel shut out of Canaan by unbelief (1 Cor 10:11).

themes: sinners perish by their own fault

schools.summary: The highways = the calling of the Gentiles, the despised poor of the by-ways (diexodous); Lightfoot on the custom of inviting poor travellers.
Clarke: the rejection of the Jews and call of the Gentiles; God sends salvation to every soul that all may be saved (Acts 13:46).
Calvin: the Gentiles called to provoke Israel to jealousy (Deut 32:21; Romans 11:17), with a warning that the Church too may be cut off if it does not answer the call.

outliers.note: Turns the calling of the Gentiles into a warning to the Church: the natural branches were not spared, and neither shall we be if we refuse the call.

schools.summary: Clarke reads the mixed gathering as the visible Church, gathering the Lord's images — floor, field, net, house, fold (Matthew 3:12; 13:26,48; 25:1,33).
Ellicott (with the dragnet, Matthew 13:47): the universal offer and the mixed visible Church; Robertson notes the 'bride-chamber filled' reading; Barnes: none good by nature.
Gill: the apostles turning from Jews to Gentiles (Paul and Barnabas).

outliers.note: Comments here on the parallel parable of the king and his debtor, off the wedding-feast thread.

schools.summary: The Oriental custom of provided festal robes makes the man's common dress a wilful insult; Ellicott reads the garment as holiness (Hebrews 12:14), rejecting narrower answers; Barnes as Christ's salvation refused.
Calvin: the faith-or-works dispute is useless (they are inseparable); the garment is freely supplied, and the man is condemned for being found in his pollution.
Henry: a frame of heart and course of life agreeable to the gospel (Ephesians 4:1; Revelation 19:8).
Wesley: the righteousness of Christ, first imputed, then implanted; Cyril urges catechumens to wash their robes in repentance.

sides.position: Holiness / a life agreeable to the gospel
The righteousness of Christ (imputed, then implanted)
A useless distinction — faith and works are inseparable; freely supplied by God

schools.summary: 'Friend' (hetaire) = companion, not a term of affection; 'speechless' (ephimōthē) = muzzled, the hypocrite self-convicted, the same verb as the silenced Sadducees in v.34.

Clarke reads the man as refuting absolute reprobation — Christ died for him, the Spirit strove, he might have been saved; Wesley: the garment is Christ's righteousness, the scene is the last day, not the Supper.
Henry urges self-examination: 'How came I in hither? Have I a wedding garment?'

outliers.note: Uses the speechless guest as an argument against absolute reprobation: the man could have been saved, and the fault is wholly his own.

schools.summary: Henry: the parable slides into the reality it figures — the binding is the helplessness of the condemned, the tares bound for the fire (Matthew 13:41), the outer darkness the night of damnation.

Ellicott notes 'take him away' is absent from the best MSS. and draws the contrast of lit hall and outer midnight; Robertson on the blacker darkness; Barnes refers it to the hypocrite's future punishment.
Gill: the binding servants are the ministers, or rather the angels who bind the tares for burning (Matthew 13:41).

Barnes: the saying is the moral of the whole parable, not of the garment alone; Robertson on klētoi/eklektoi; Ellicott: the chosen accept and comply, election (1 Peter 1:2) following but not here in view.
Calvin restrains speculation on eternal election: outward profession is no proof God will own us; let each examine himself (2 Timothy 2:19).
Origen joins the saying to the narrow gate (Luke 13:24; Matthew 7:14) and his doctrine of the gates of Hades.
Clarke from the Roman militia muster ('make sure work for eternity'); Wesley: many hear, few believe; many of the visible, few of the invisible Church.

outliers.note: Reads the few chosen through the narrow gate and his recurring figure of the gates of Hades.

schools.summary: Pseudo-Chrysostom's dammed-stream image of dogged malice; Chrysostom: Pharisees' disciples and Herod's men sent together so any answer would be faulted.
Jerome supplies the political background — Judaea's tribute under Augustus, the Pharisees holding God's people exempt, the Herodians paying Rome.
Henry: the rulers take counsel against him (Psalm 2:2), to make him an offender for a word and trap him between the multitude and Rome.
The alliance of natural enemies (Pharisees and Herodians) against Christ; foes united only in opposing him.

outliers.note: Likens the persistent malice of the Jews to a stream that, dammed at one outlet, finds another channel.

schools.summary: The Gloss reads the preface as a triple guard against the three ways of failing to teach truth; Jerome: a smooth and treacherous enquiry to set God against Caesar.

Henry: their words of Christ were true (faithful Teacher, bold Reprover) but their intent all flattery and treachery — hatred covered with deceit (Proverbs 26:23), atheism to cheat the heart-searcher (Revelation 2:23).
The Herodians = the Galilean Sadducees / Herod's party allied with the Pharisees; the flattery designed to make him speak freely and unguardedly.
Calvin: feigned reverence because they dared not openly assail him.

Henry: the dilemma (Pharisees or Herodians offended either way); the tribute proved the sceptre departed from Judah (Genesis 49:10) and Shiloh come; their scruple a proud old mistake (John 8:33; Jeremiah 27:12).
Ellicott: a live daily question, so 'What thinkest thou?' wore the look of an honest appeal.
Gill: they hoped either answer would expose him to Jews or Romans, triumphing as Pharaoh over hemmed-in Israel.
Barnes and Ellicott: He answered their intent, not their speech, reading their thoughts and exposing the mask with the word 'hypocrites.'
Pseudo-Chrysostom: God answers hearts not words; Christ shames them to save them, since His wrath profits more than man's favour.
Calvin: the rebuke unmasks a settled malice, the question put by cunning, not from desire to learn.
The coin is the Roman denarius, the tribute-penny bearing Tiberius's image; the very token of the subjection the Pharisees resented.
Jerome: Christ asks not from ignorance but to confute the tempters out of their own words; He must have known whose image was on the coin.
Calvin/Gill: wisdom fixes the argument on a visible fact in their own hands; handling the coin already acknowledges Caesar's authority.
The coin's 'image and superscription' (the emperor's head and titles) make the questioners name Caesar themselves, the verdict coming from their own mouths.
Lightfoot: the prevailing coinage is a token of sovereignty; using Caesar's money already acknowledges his order.
Hilary: Caesar's image is on the coin, God's image on us — give Caesar money, God body, soul and will; Jerome: Christ himself paid tribute and did the Father's will.
Chrysostom: render to Caesar only what is not opposed to religion; what is opposed becomes 'the Devil's tribute,' not Caesar's.
Origen reads it morally — necessaries to the body (Caesar), the soul's goods to God — censuring both Pharisaic over-rigour (1 Tim 4:3) and Herodian indulgence.
Civil dues to the civil power, supreme allegiance to God, the two in their proper orders, not in conflict.

outliers.note: Allegorises Caesar as the body's due (or the prince of this world), God as the soul's, balancing rigour and indulgence.

schools.summary: Jerome: they wondered at His wisdom but carried away 'their unbelief and wonder together' — astonishment short of faith.
To marvel at Christ yet leave him is the tragedy of the merely convinced; the snare broken by one saying, the assaults not yet over.
Calvin: what silenced them ought to have subdued them, but obstinate men withdraw rather than yield to the truth.
Jerome distinguishes the sects: the Sadducees deny resurrection, angel and spirit (Acts 23:8), receiving only the Pentateuch.
Origen: they deny the soul's immortality too; the 'power of God' they know not is Christ himself (1 Cor 1:24).
Chrysostom on the levirate frame of the trap; Pseudo-Chrysostom: the devil raised the sect to break down righteous striving by removing the hope of resurrection.
Henry: the Sadducees as Epicurean materialists; the gospel suffers between hypocrites and infidels confederate against Christ.
Lightfoot: a stock cavil ('the cloud passeth, so the dead return not') with which they had often puzzled the Pharisees.

outliers.note: Reads the Sadducees' denial as the devil's device to undo righteous striving by removing the hope of resurrection.

schools.summary: The levirate statute (Deuteronomy 25:5; Ruth 4:5), a political law to preserve families and inheritances, made the platform of the Sadducees' trap.
Chrysostom: the levirate marriage was an alleviation of death, that the dead man's name not perish, established by the nearest kin.
Henry: the brothers obeyed though they might refuse (Deut 25:7) — be governed by the rule, not the event; the case real or hypothetical is immaterial.
The seven successive childless husbands framed to push the objection to its sharpest, so no single claim could be preferred in a risen life.
Henry: the case shows death's desolations in families (Job 18:19) — except the Lord build the house — and the brothers' obedience to the law.
The repetition (second to seventh) completes the single device of the objection — one woman, seven husbands, no issue — heaping up the apparent difficulty.
Gill registers the successive marriages as the continuation of the Sadducees' constructed case.
The woman's death completes the case; all claims now stand on equal footing for the question to come.
Henry: survivorship is but a reprieve; death's cup goes round (Jeremiah 25:26) — the fable unwittingly rehearses death's dominion.
Henry: the trap assumes the risen life reproduces this one's marriages (the 'Platonic year'); the carnal Pharisaic notion of the resurrection gave the Sadducees their opening.
The seven equal claims meant to make the resurrection ridiculous; the snare turns wholly on a carnal picture of the world to come.
Henry: ignorance is the cause of error; they know not God's power (which makes resurrection credible, Acts 26:8) nor the Scriptures (which affirm it — Job 19:26; Ezekiel 37; Daniel 12:2).
Barnes and Ellicott: error springs from a twofold ignorance — of the word that promises and the power that effects the resurrection.
Origen: the 'power of God' is Christ himself, the agent of resurrection, unknown to them with the Scriptures that spoke of him.
Henry: marriage, necessary now, has no place in the resurrection, where God is all in all and the spiritual body has no carnal desire nor need to recruit the race (Malachi 2:15).
Pseudo-Chrysostom: marriage replenishes what death consumes, so where death ends so does birth; the angelic comparison fits the ceasing of marriage, chastity being an angelic act.
Hilary and Jerome: 'as the angels' assures a spiritual heavenly life, said of beings capable of marriage who yet shall not marry.
Calvin/Wesley: the risen state transcends earthly conditions, the relations of this world yielding to the vision of God.
The pointed 'have ye not read' reproaches men proud of the Law; the proof drawn from Moses, whom the Sadducees revered, not the rejected prophets.
Jerome's principle: plainer texts (Isaiah 26:19; Daniel 12:2) would be useless against men who denied their authority, so Christ meets them on Mosaic ground.

Jerome: Christ proves from Moses (whom they accept) that souls abide after death, whence the resurrection of bodies follows; Hilary: the dead cannot possess the eternal, so the patriarchs live; Augustine: the flesh's matter is restored.
Origen: the repeated 'the God of' each patriarch shows each possessed God entirely; the 'I am that I am' (Exodus 3:14) cannot be God of those who are not.
The covenant relation proves the soul's continued life immediately and the body's resurrection by consequence — God of the living, not the dead.

outliers.note: Presses the exact grammar — 'the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob' — to show each possessed God wholly.

schools.summary: Remigius: the multitudes, not the Sadducees, were astonished — as still in the Church the faithful rejoice when adversaries are overcome; Raban: Christ answered aptly against their unbelief.
The crowd astonished at the doctrine — the decisive proof of resurrection drawn from a text the Sadducees themselves accepted.
Jerome: hate and jealousy are the parents of impudence; the foes unite to tempt Jesus after both were confuted.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: they gather to silence by numbers what reasoning could not; Chrysostom: their envy and lovelessness put forward the lawyer to try him.
Henry: they are vexed that Christ was honoured rather than glad the Sadducees were silenced — envy that sacrifices a public good to private pique.
The regathering opens the last testing question, after which none dared ask him more.
Henry: the lawyer a casuist, tempting not to ensnare but to test, the man Mark says was 'not far from the kingdom' (Mark 12:34).
Augustine reconciles Matthew's 'tempting' with Mark's commendation: either the answer corrected him, or the tempting was a cautious testing, not enmity.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: he asks the greatest commandment having kept the least; he should seek higher righteousness only who has fulfilled the lower.
Origen: the lawyer a type of all who question a teacher to try, not to learn, calling Christ 'Master' yet refusing him.

debates.question: Was the lawyer a hardened tempter or a near-sincere enquirer?

sides.position: A cautious or half-sincere tester, brought near the kingdom
A hardened tempter who kept the least while asking the greatest

schools.summary: Henry: a needless question since all God's commands are great (Hosea 8:12), yet a real rabbinic dispute; designed to try his judgment, that magnifying one he might seem to vilify the rest.
Jerome: he asks to have occasion to cavil whatever the answer, since all God commands is great.
The man asks the supreme commandment; the answer will name not one precept but the principle from which all flow.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: love not fear (sons, not slaves); heart = inclining to none above God, soul = the mind stayed on truth, mind = all the faculties open for Him.
Augustine: heart = thoughts, soul = life, mind = understanding, all given to God; no part of life left unfilled of Him; the Gloss: think, will, remember nothing contrary to Him.
Origen reads the measures as recollection, readiness to die for God, and bringing forth nothing but what is of God.
The threefold phrase demands a love whole and undivided — sincere, strong, and supreme.
Origen: 'first and great' means not first in scriptural order but in supremacy of value, with lesser commandments down to the least.
The love of God is first in dignity, not sequence — the spring and foundation of all the rest, acceptable obedience flowing only from it.
Henry: the summary of the first table; God being infinitely good is to be loved first, nothing loved but for him.

outliers.note: Infers from 'first and great' a graded hierarchy of commandments down to the least, and requires the three conditions to truly keep it.

schools.summary: Augustine: 'neighbour' embraces every man, the angels, even Christ the good Samaritan; love self and neighbour for God's sake; Hilary: the likeness is identity of obligation and reward.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: loving man is like loving God, for man is God's image, as a king is honoured in his statue.
Henry: a natural, sanctified self-love is the rule of the duty; love neighbour as truly as self (1 John 4:20).
Love to neighbour is the sum of the second table, flowing from and evidencing love to God.

outliers.note: Extends 'neighbour' to every man, the angels, and Christ himself as the good Samaritan.

schools.summary: Augustine: 'hang' = refer to as their end; the selfsame love loves God for Himself and neighbour for God; Raban: the two tables of the decalogue; Hilary: the obligation of both alike.
Henry: obedience begins in the affections; love fulfils the law (Romans 13:10), a short sweet word making the yoke easy.
The two commandments are the foundation of all the rest — great not by excluding but by including the others.
Christ turns questioner; Ellicott: from now they fall back on treachery; Barnes: a threefold aim — to show their ignorance, humble them, and teach the Messiah's superiority to David.
Henry: they questioned him from the law, he questions them from the promise; many full of the law forget Christ (Isaiah 8:9).
Calvin: with death approaching Christ attests his divinity, that the godly may rely on him.

'The Christ' = the Messiah; Robertson: the question touches his Deity and Humanity; Ellicott: the Pharisees give the traditional 'son of David' without asking if it is the whole truth; Clarke: the genealogies, now lost, once verified the prophecy.
Henry turns it inward — 'What think ye of Christ?' — precious to believers, nothing to others.
Calvin: the Pharisees expected a merely human son of David; Satan stripped Christ's divinity, abolishing the hope of eternal life.
Ellicott: David wrote Psalm 110 by a higher Spirit, of the far-off Christ, with consensus in Judaism and apostolic use (Acts 2:34); 'in spirit' = by inspiration (Acts 2:30).
Calvin: the psalm is not of David but prophetic of Christ's reign and new priesthood (Psalm 110:4); David, head of the people, calls this one Lord, so he is more than man.
Henry: David in spirit calls him Lord (1 Cor 12:3); unbelievers cannot clear the incongruity of a father calling his son Lord — the Messiah is God.
Clarke: 'Jehovah said unto my Adoni' — the divine name and a title of dominion; a prophetic declaration of the Messiah; the right hand and footstool = exaltation and subjection of foes (1 Cor 15:25).
Calvin: a song of triumph — Christ's kingdom set up by God is invincible; the right hand embraces heaven and earth, his power for ever unimpaired.
Wesley: a dominion to which David himself was subject; to sit at the right hand is the highest authority and power.

outliers.note: Marshals the apostolic texts on the Son's session at the right hand (Acts 2:34; Romans 8:34; Ephesians 1:20; Hebrews 1:3).

schools.summary: Barnes/Clarke: if David's Lord and superior, existing in David's time, how his son? Only resolved by the Messiah being Divine as well as human; the Jews, unable to deny the conclusion, were confounded.
Henry: hold fast that he is David's Lord and by that explain his being David's son — Root by deity, Offspring by manhood (Revelation 22:16).
Henry: they were puzzled by ignorance or impiety, the Messiah's deity being the only key; God silences many he does not save — convinced but not converted, they withdraw.
The verse is the verdict on all the day's assaults — Pharisees, Herodians, Sadducees, lawyer each confounded; from now the contest turns from debate to plot.
